segments.s620-g49: This clause states a general prohibition/exclusion. نباشد is a classical negative present/subjunctive copula used here existentially ('there is not' / 'let there not be'); گذر is the noun 'passage' and is the grammatical subject; جز ... به introduces the limiting phrase 'except by/on the command of the king' where به فرمان شاه is an ablative/agent-like expression ('by the king's command'). Word order is typical older Persian: negative copula + subject + exception phrase.
segments.s620-g50: همان refers back to that very thing (the king's command); نیز is an emphatic 'also/indeed'. جیحون is the proper name of the river (Amu Darya). می‌انجی is a present-tense verb with the prefix می- marking habitual/present aspect; here it functions transitively to mean something like 'makes/causes to go or be put together/turned' (the exact lemma sense given in the list doesn't match this usage, so the dictionary senses provided do not cover the verbal meaning used). به راه is a directional complement 'into/on the road/way.' Overall: 'that (order) also causes the Jeyhun to be put on the way.'
segments.s620-g51: به لشکر is a locative/locution 'in the army' (به often marks location or motion toward); یکی مرد is a noun phrase 'one man / a certain man' (یکی here functions as 'a certain'); بد shمر نام is an apposition: بد qualifies مرد ('bad/wicked man') and شمر is the proper name; نام at the end is functioning parenthetically as 'named' or 'whose name was.' The word order (locative fronting, then noun phrase, then appellation) is typical of epic style.
segments.s620-g52: This short verbless clause lists attributes of that man: خردمند 'wise' (predicate adjective); و ... و link three nouns introduced by با ('with') which here means 'possessing' or 'endowed with.' گوهر is literal 'gem' but in context can mean 'valuable quality/possessions'; رای here means 'counsel/opinion' (classical sense of judgment/counsel); کام is 'desire/wish.' The sequence is a poetic catalogue of qualities or possessions attached to the man.
segments.s620-g53: مر is a dative pronoun 'to/for me' placed before the rest of the clause for emphasis; او را is the direct object 'him' (را marks definiteness/object). به توران زمین is a prepositional phrase 'over the land of Turan' (به here marks the domain/extent); شاه کرد is the verbal complex 'made (into) king' — کرد is the simple past of 'to do/make' used causatively here. Word order is flexible in epic Persian; the dative (مر) fronted gives a flavour of emphasis or beneficiary.
segments.s620-g54: سر تخت 'the top/summit of the throne' (سر as 'top' modifying تخت 'throne'), او is the possessive 'his' following the noun (classical placement), and افسر ماه کرد is another resultative construction: افسر 'helmet/diadem' qualified by ماه 'moon' (a common poetic epithet meaning 'moonlike, beautiful, or shining'), then کرد 'made' — so 'he made the throne's summit a moonlike diadem' or, more naturally, 'he set a moonlike diadem on the top of his throne.' The verb کرد repeats the causative/making action.
segments.s620-g55: همان 'that/that very' identifies the object (the same crown just referred to); تاج زرینش is a noun phrase 'his golden crown' (زرینش = زرین + possessive -ش 'his'); بر سر is the prepositional phrase 'upon (his) head' and نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'to put, place.' The verb is perfective (past) and completes the action of placing the crown on the head.
segments.s620-g56: همه شهر توران is a genitive/nominal phrase 'all the cities of Turan' (همه 'all' quantifies شهر 'cities'); بدو is a dative pronoun 'to him' meaning 'for him' or 'on his account'; گشت is the past tense of گشتن used copularly 'became' and شاد is the predicate adjective 'happy/joyful.' The clause therefore means 'all the cities of Turan became joyful for him.'
segments.s620-g41: چو introduces a temporal clause ('when'); ازین کار is a prepositional phrase ('in/with regard to this matter') where ازین = از + این (an older orthography) and کار = 'affair/matter'. کام او شد روا is a verbless/result clause rendered with شد (become) plus روا 'fitting/permissible' to mean 'his desire was fulfilled/it was proper for his wish to be granted.'
segments.s620-g42: The clause states a consequent action: بستد is a past-tense transitive verb 'he shut/stilled/caused to cease'. باژ (a cry/noise) is the direct object. ابا here functions as a verbal element meaning 'to withhold/refuse/put an end to' (an archaic usage), and ز ترکان نوا is an ablative/agent phrase 'the sound/cry from the Turks' (ز = 'from'). The overall sense is that he caused the Turks' cry to cease.
segments.s620-g43: چو ... آمد is another temporal clause ('when he returned and came'); برگشت and آمد are two verbs presenting the same event (returned/arrived). به شهر is the destination 'to the city'. فرب is a predicate adjective describing his state on arrival ('fat, plump, well‑fed'); its placement after شهر is metrical/poetic, so it modifies the subject rather than the city.
segments.s620-g44: پر از is a fixed idiom 'full of'; رنگ رخسار is a genitive phrase 'the colour/complexion of the face' (= a flushed or rosy countenance). و links the two coordinated predicative phrases; پرخنده لب is a compound adjective phrase 'with lips full of laughter/smiles' (پر used again as intensifier). The whole describes his cheerful, healthy appearance.
segments.s620-g45: برآسود is a past-state verb 'he was at rest/was relieved'. هفته (with an understood یک) gives the duration 'for a week'. نراند is the past form of راند with the negative prefix ن- here rendering 'did not drive/send out'; لشکر is the direct object 'the army/host' that he did not dispatch.
segments.s620-g46: ز چین is an ablative or source phrase 'from the ranks/lines' (چین = row/fold/line). مهتران را is the definite object 'the chiefs' (را marking them), همه 'all' emphasizes totality, پیش here means 'before/in the presence of (the king)', and خواند is the verb 'he called/summoned'. Together: 'he summoned all the chiefs forward from the ranks.'
segments.s620-g47: برآورد here is a past verb meaning 'he set up/erected' (an accomplishment). میلی is the object 'a milestone/mile-marker'. ز سنگ و ز گج lists materials or kinds ('of stone and of gaj'); ز functions poetically as 'of' or 'from'. Note that گج is an archaic word whose precise material sense is context-dependent.
segments.s620-g48: که introduces a result/purpose clause ('such that/so that'). کس را is the (indefinite) person marked by را, به ایران 'to Iran' marks the direction/target, and ز ترک و خلج specifies the group 'from the Turks and the Khalaj'. The clause therefore indicates a consequence or intended effect concerning people of those groups entering Iran (the remainder of the clause continues after this line).
segments.s620-g33: دل شاه بهرام is a noun phrase with the possessor following the possessed (literal: 'heart (دل) of King Bahram'); زیشان is the archaic contracted prepositional phrase زِ ایشان meaning 'because of / for them' or 'from them'; بسوخت is a simple past intransitive verb (ب + سوخت) meaning 'burned' or 'was burned' describing the state of the heart. The order is possessum–possessor then the circumstantial phrase and the past verb, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s620-g34: به دست خرد is an instrumental/agent phrase ('by the hand of wisdom' — به + دست + noun in genitive sense), where خرد is an abstract noun 'wisdom'; چشم خشمش is a genitive construction 'the eye of his anger' (خشم + possessive suffix -ش); بدوخت is a past-tense verb 'sewed/stitched' (ب + دوخت). Together the phrase means 'by wisdom's hand, the eye of his anger was sewn' (poetic way to say his anger was restrained).
segments.s620-g35: ز خون ریختن is a prepositional/participial phrase where ز (poetic) = 'from' and خون ریختن = 'shedding blood'; دست‌گردان is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'the fickle‑handed' or 'those with wandering hands'; ببست is the past tense 'he bound/closed/restrained' (ب + بست). The whole clause means he restrained those inclined to spill blood.
segments.s620-g36: پراندیشه is a single adjectival/nominal word meaning 'full of thought, pensive'; شد is the past of شدن 'became'; شاه یزدان‌پرست is the noun phrase 'the God‑worshipping king' (شاه = 'king', یزدان‌پرست = 'pious/God‑worshipping'). Word order places the predicate (پراندیشه شد) before the identifying noun phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s620-g37: چو here functions as 'when'; مهر جهاندار is a genitive phrase 'the sovereign's affection' (مهر = 'affection, love'; جهاندار = 'world‑holder/sovereign'); پیوسته شد is a verbal phrase 'became continuous/continued' (پیوسته = 'constant/continuous', شد = 'became'). The clause means 'when the ruler's affection continued.'
segments.s620-g38: دل مرد is 'the man's/hero's heart' (دل + مرد where مرد takes the sense 'valiant man' in epic context); آشفته is an adjective 'disturbed/troubled'; آهسته شد is 'became slow/soft/quiet' (آهسته often idiomatically meaning 'calmed' here) with شد as the copula. The sequence gives 'the valiant man's troubled heart became calm.'
segments.s620-g39: بر شاه شد is a phrase meaning 'became over the king' or 'was set upon/over the king' (بر = 'on/over', شد = 'became/was placed'); مهتر مهتران is an emphatic nominal phrase 'the chief, (chief of chiefs)' — poetic apposition/intensification where the first noun names the office and the second amplifies it. The line uses inverted order: locative + copula + predicate noun.
segments.s620-g40: بپذرفت is the simple past 'he accepted' (ب + پذرفت); هر سال is the temporal phrase 'every year'; باژ گران is a two‑word nominal phrase where باژ is a poetic word (contextually 'tribute/cry/noise') and گران is the adjective 'heavy/valuable'. The clause reads 'he accepted each year the heavy …' (i.e., heavy tribute/noise — باژ is a poetic term not fully represented in the provided senses).
segments.s620-g17: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/among.' ترکان is the plural subject group 'the Turks.' هرانکس که introduces an indefinite relative clause 'whoever/anyone who'; که is the relative marker. بد is used adverbially ('badly/ill') and combines with پیش رو (lit. 'before/face') to mean 'having behaved ill in (someone's) presence' or 'ill‑behaving toward (him).' Grammatically this whole segment is an indefinite nominal phrase ('among the Turks, whoever...') that will be continued or completed by the next segment.
segments.s620-g18: ز again functions as 'from/among.' پیران is 'the elders' and و simply coordinates 'and.' خنجرگزاران is a compound plural 'dagger‑bearers' (i.e. those who bear daggers, often assassins or armed retainers); نو follows and qualifies it as 'new' or 'newly appointed.' This segment continues the enumeration begun in the previous segment (so both groups are being listed).
segments.s620-g19: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone.' پیش + name (پیش بهرام) means 'in the presence of Bahram' or 'before Bahram.' رفتند is the past tense 3rd‑person plural 'they went.' خوار is a post‑verbal predicate/adjective meaning 'humiliated/despised' describing their state as they went; Persian often places such predicative adjectives after the verb.
segments.s620-g20: پیاده indicates manner 'on foot' (or 'the footmen'). پر + از expresses 'full of' or 'covered with' (here پر از خون = 'covered with blood'). دل with خاکسار forms a verbless descriptive phrase 'دل خاکسار' = 'with contrite/penitent hearts' (خاکسار as an adjective/noun meaning 'humble; contrite'). The whole segment gives manner and condition of those who went.
segments.s620-g21: که here introduces what they said or pleaded (a reported clause). شاها is a vocative 'O King!' Rدا and بلند اخترا form a coordinated/compounded object: ردا 'robe/robe of state' and بلند (an adjective 'lofty') modifies اخترا (an archaic/poetic noun here meaning a kind of honor or distinction). The phrase functions as the petition or formula they address to the king.
segments.s620-g22: بر is the preposition 'upon/on.' آزادگان is the plural 'the free (people).' جهان is 'the world' and here forms a genitival frame 'the free of the world.' مهترا appears as a vocative or honorific appended to the line (an epithet/ name: 'Mahtara' or 'radiant lady'), addressing or invoking a person in the formula. Together the segment specifies the recipients of the robe and lofty distinction.
segments.s620-g23: گر is the conditional 'if.' ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning roughly 'henceforth/now' or marking the time of the hypothetical; it sets the temporal/qualifying frame for the condition. خاقان is the subject 'the khaghan' (an imperial title) and گنهکار is a predicate adjective 'sinful, a sinner.' گشت is the past‑tense 'became' (3rd‑person singular), linking the predicate to the subject.
segments.s620-g24: ز here means 'from/because of' or 'away from' in the sense 'alienated from.' عهد is 'oath/pledge/covenant' and جهاندار 'the world‑ruler/sovereign' forms a possessive/genitival phrase 'the covenant of the sovereign.' بیزار is a predicate adjective 'averse/loath' and گشت again is 'became' — together 'became averse to the sovereign's covenant.'
segments.s620-g1: بیاسود is a past/predicative form meaning 'was at rest' placed before the locative and name for poetic word order; در مرو is a locative prepositional phrase 'in Merv'; بهرام‌گور is the subject (proper name) coming last, a common inversion in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s620-g2: چو is the poetic subordinating conjunction 'when'; آسوده شد is a predicate made of the adjective آسوده + شد 'became/was at ease' (past); شاه و جنگی ستور is a coordinated subject 'the king and the war-steed' with جنگی qualifying ستور ('war-steed').
segments.s620-g3: ز is the causal/ablative preposition 'because of/from'; تیزی 'swiftness' is the cause, روانش ('his حرکت/flow/motion') carries the possessive suffix -ش; مدارا گزید is a verb phrase 'chose moderation/forbearance' with گزید as the past-tense verb 'chose'. Word order places cause before the verb.
segments.s620-g4: دلش is 'his heart' (possessed noun) functioning as subject; رای رزم is a nominal phrase 'plan/decision for battle' where رزم qualifies رای; بخارا is the place chosen (direct object/goal), and گزید is the past verb 'chose' finishing the clause (typical SOV/predicate-final order in poetry).
segments.s620-g5: The initial به یک روز و یک شب is an adverbial temporal phrase 'in one day and one night' where به functions as an adverbial/time marker; به آموی is the directional 'to his uncle' (here به = 'to/into'); شد is the past/inchoative verb 'became/arrived' used poetically to indicate that he reached his uncle.
segments.s620-g6: ز as causal 'from/because of'; نخچیر و بازی are coordinated nouns 'hunting (game) and sport/play'; جهانجوی شد is the predicate 'he became world-seeking/ambitious' with شد as the past auxiliary meaning 'became'.
segments.s620-g7: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'; ز آموی is the source 'from his uncle' (ز + possessed noun); یک پاس شب is a temporal/qualifying phrase 'a single pass/turn of night' where پاس means 'a watch/turn' and شب 'night' follows it.
segments.s620-g8: گذر کرد is a periphrastic past 'he passed/crossed' (noun گذر + auxiliary کرد); بر expresses movement 'over/across'; آب و ریگ list the surfaces crossed 'water and sand'; فرب is a postpositive adjective modifying ریگ ('rich/abundant' sand) — adjective-after-noun ordering is common in older poetic diction.
segments.s620-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. The subject خورشید (the sun) precedes a noun phrase روی هوا ('the face/face-surface of the air' or more idiomatically 'the air/sky'), and کرد is the past tense verb 'made/caused' (here 'turned'). The adjective زرد follows the verb as a predicative complement: 'made (it) yellow.' This is a poetic, slightly condensed word order where the cause-verb کرد takes the predicative adjective after it.
segments.s620-g10: بینداخت is a past transitive verb 'threw/cast' (third-person); the agent is understood to be the same subject as the previous clause (the sun) though not repeated. پیراهن (tunic/robe) is the direct object, followed by لاژورد which functions as a color/qualifier ('lapis/azure'). This is a concise poetic structure: Verb + Object + Modifier.
segments.s620-g11: زمانه شد is 'the time/age became' (شد = became). از گرد uses از as an ablative 'from' and گرد here means 'dust', so 'became from dust' — followed by a simile introduced by چون: چون پر چرغ = 'like the feather (پر) of a lamp (چرغ)'. In short, a poetic simile: 'the time grew like a lamp's feather/dust.'
segments.s620-g12: جهانجوی is a nominative agent 'the world-seeker' or 'ambitious one' and بگذشت is the past intransitive verb 'passed by / went past.' The preposition بر marks what is passed over (بر مای و مرغ = 'over wine and bird[s]'), so the clause says the world-seeker passed beyond/abstained from wine and birds (poetically: drinking and song). The coordination مای و مرغ links the two objects.
segments.s620-g13: همه لشکر ترک is the full subject 'all the Turkish army/host.' The verb phrase بر هم زدند uses بر with هم + زدند (past plural of زدن) to mean 'fell upon / attacked together' — a common idiom in which بر...زدن conveys striking/attacking. هم here emphasizes 'together' or mutual action.
segments.s620-g14: به بوم و به دشت is a pair of prepositional phrases 'into/to the homeland and to the plain' (به = toward/into). آتش اندر زدند is a poetic way of saying 'they struck fire within' — i.e. they kindled or set fire to those places; اندر is the archaic preposition/adverb 'in/within'. The verb زدند again expresses violent action (they set/struck).
segments.s620-g15: ستاره is the subject 'the star.' همی is a classical continuity/intensifying particle (here carrying an immediacy/iterative sense). دامن ماه is a genitival phrase 'the moon's hem/skirt/edge' functioning as the object, and جست is the past of جهیدن 'leapt/sprang.' The word order (Subject — modifier/object — Verb) is typical of Persian poetry, and the verb indicates a sudden motion.
segments.s620-g16: پدر بر پسر presents two participants ('father' and 'son') with the preposition بر introducing the second; in poetry بر can serve to link or mark items in sequence. بر همی راه جست: بر همی (together/one upon the other) + راه جست 'sprang onto / set out on the road' (جست = leapt/ran). Thus the clause means that father and son together rushed onto the road. The construction is concise and typical of epic diction where conjunctions are compressed.
segments.s620-g25: به دستت is a prepositional phrase with the possessive/agent suffix -ت on دست; it can mean either “into your hand/to your power” or idiomatically “by your hand.” گرفتار شد is a past periphrastic: گرفتـار (adjective/participle “caught/ensnared”) + شد (“became/was”), so together “was caught/was taken.” بی‌گمان is an adverb (“undoubtedly”) modifying the whole clause and follows the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s620-g26: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” بشکست is a 3rd‑person past form meaning “broke” (here of a covenant); in poetry the agent may be omitted so the clause can be read either active (“the king broke the covenant”) or passive/impersonal (“the covenant was broken”). پیمان شاه جهان is a genitive/possessive sequence (pīman + shah + jahān); classical Persian often omits explicit ezāfe between these nouns in verse.
segments.s620-g27: تو is the 2nd‑person pronoun addressing the addressee. خون سر بیگناهان is a noun phrase meaning literally “the blood of the heads of the innocent” (i.e. the innocent’s blood); classical word order places the head noun before its possessor/qualifier in poetry. مریز is the negative imperative “do not spill/let fall” (present imperative with negative meaning).
segments.s620-g28: نه is a sentential negation placed before the predicate. خوب آید is an impersonal or evaluative construction with آید (“comes/happens”) — literally “good comes” (i.e. “it is not good”). از + نامداران introduces the source/partner (“from/with the renowned”), and ستیز is a noun “strife/contending”; together: “no good comes from contending with the famous.”
segments.s620-g29: گر is the conditional particle “if.” از ما همی باژ خواهی is a conditional protasis: از ما (“from us”), همی is an archaic/emphatic particle (here reinforcing the verb), باژ (“a cry/alarm”) is the object, and خواهی is 2nd‑person present/future “you want/you ask.” رواست is composed of the adjective روا (“permissible/fitting”) plus the copula (stressed here), yielding “it is permitted/it is fitting.”
segments.s620-g30: سر بیگناهان بریدن is an infinitival/nominalized verb phrase—literally “to cut off the heads of the innocent” (سر = head, بیگناهان = the innocent, بریدن = the infinitive ‘to sever/behead’). چراست is a rhetorical/interrogative predicate here (“why is it?” / “what right is there?”), so the clause asks why innocent heads should be cut.
segments.s620-g31: همه مرد و زن is a distributive phrase “all men and women.” بندگان is the predicate noun “servants/subjects,” and توایم is an archaic first‑person predicative form meaning roughly “I am yours” (here used collectively: “we/are yours”). The order places the universal subject first, then the predicative phrase and copula.
segments.s620-g32: به رزم اندر is a doubled locative/temporal phrase (“to/into battle, in/among”), with اندر an archaic preposition reinforcing location. افگندگان is an agentive/plural participial noun from افگندن (literally “throwers/those who cast”); syntactically it names those engaged in or sent into fighting. توایم again is the copular/predicative form meaning “I/we are yours,” so the clause says in battle we are the ones you cast/send into the fight.

sentences.s620-s25: There is no passage except by the king's command; even that (command) makes the Jeyhun turn into the road.
sentences.s620-s26: In the army was a certain man — called Shimir (Shamr) the wicked.
sentences.s620-s27: He made him king over the land of Turan (for me).
sentences.s620-s28: He placed that golden crown upon his head; all the cities of Turan became joyful for him.
sentences.s620-s21: When his desire in that matter was fulfilled, he silenced the Turks' cry.
sentences.s620-s22: When he returned to the city he arrived plump, his face flushed with colour and his lips full of smiles.
sentences.s620-s23: He rested for a week, did not send forth the army, and summoned all the chiefs before him from the ranks.
sentences.s620-s24: He erected a milestone of stone and of gaj, so that none from the Turks and the Khalaj ...
sentences.s620-s17: King Bahram's heart burned for them; by the hand of wisdom the eye of his anger was sewn (i.e., his rage was restrained).
sentences.s620-s18: He checked the fickle‑handed from shedding blood; the God‑worshipping king became thoughtful.
sentences.s620-s19: When the sovereign's affection remained steady, the valiant man's troubled heart was calmed.
sentences.s620-s20: A foremost chief of chiefs was set over the king; he accepted every year the heavy (tribute/noise).
sentences.s620-s9: From the Turks — whoever had behaved ill in their presence — and from the elders and the newly‑made dagger‑bearers,
sentences.s620-s10: All went before Bahram humbled; the footmen, drenched in blood, their hearts contrite.
sentences.s620-s11: — saying, 'O King, robe and a lofty distinction upon the free of the world, O Mahtara.'
sentences.s620-s12: If henceforth the khaghan became a sinner, he became averse to the sovereign's covenant.
sentences.s620-s1: Bahram Gur rested in Merv; when the king and his war-steed were at ease.
sentences.s620-s2: Because of his swiftness he chose moderation; his heart chose Bukhara as the plan for battle.
sentences.s620-s3: In the space of a day and a night he came to his uncle; from hunting and sport he became a seeker of the world.
sentences.s620-s4: He came from his uncle on a single night's watch; he passed over water and rich sand.
sentences.s620-s5: When the sun turned the air yellow, it cast off an azure robe.
sentences.s620-s6: The times became like dust, like a lamp's feather; the world-seeker passed over wine and song-birds.
sentences.s620-s7: All the Turkish host fell upon (attacked); they set fire to the homesteads and the plains.
sentences.s620-s8: The star leapt at the moon's hem; father and son together sprang onto the road.
sentences.s620-s13: He was certainly captured by your hand when the king of the world broke his covenant.
sentences.s620-s14: Do not spill the blood of the innocent; no good comes from contending with the renowned.
sentences.s620-s15: If you ask a cry from us, that is permitted; why should the heads of the innocent be severed?
sentences.s620-s16: All men and women are your servants; in battle we are those you throw (into the fight).
